utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive condition-based maintenance response packages that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. Both correctly link sensor anomalies to failure modes, differentiate urgency levels with COMPRESSOR-04 at the top, provide shift-differentiated operator checklists within certification boundaries, address recurring failure root causes systemically, organize outputs along TPM pillar distinctions, and explicitly adjust PM intervals based on sensor data. The wi_skills run invoked the maintenance-scheduler skill and produced a more structured TPM roadmap (tpm_implementation_roadmap.md with 8-pillar coverage, package_summary.json) with slightly tighter integration of the skill's domain methodology, while the wo_skills run independently produced an equally comprehensive package with comparable depth and specificity. In practice, the quality difference is marginal — both runs exceed the passing threshold for all items. If a distinction must be made, wi_skills demonstrates marginally stronger TPM pillar labeling and structured roadmap organization, but wo_skills performance is unexpectedly strong for a baseline run, likely because the underlying LLM has sufficient domain knowledge to replicate the skill's output quality on this task.

While wi_skills produced a higher-quality autonomous maintenance checklist (structured with acceptance criteria, per-shift differentiation, and clear pass/fail criteria — J2-03), it critically failed to address COMPRESSOR-04's immediate escalation, which is the most safety-critical element of the scenario. wo_skills explicitly escalated COMPRESSOR-04 to Priority 0 / immediate shutdown, covered all 5 equipment IDs, provided sensor-to-failure-mode mapping for all flagged assets, included systemic root cause corrections, and differentiated urgency levels across machines. The verifier results confirm this: wo_skills passed all 5 checks while wi_skills failed 2 (COMPRESSOR-04 urgency and all equipment IDs present). For a real-world reliability engineering context, the failure to escalate a critical compressor breach is a significant quality deficit that outweighs wi_skills' checklist formatting advantage.

utility_details.judge_error: In both runs, the /maintenance-scheduler binary was not found (exit code 127), so neither agent could invoke the skill executable directly. The wi_skills agent fell back to manual analysis using jq and shell scripting, while the wo_skills agent used python3. This is expected behavior per the skill framework, but the wi_skills agent's fallback analysis was substantially more thorough because it had access to the skill's analytical framework guidance.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is substantially more effective across all 7 evaluation items. The wi_skills run scored 7/7 while wo_skills scored 0/7. Key differences: (1) wi_skills correctly enforces the REACTOR-01 production constraint by placing WO-001 in the Week 3 maintenance window, while wo_skills schedules it in Week 1 in violation of constraints; (2) wi_skills computes MTBF/MTTR/availability metrics from the failure log data, which wo_skills entirely omits; (3) wi_skills provides explicit procurement triggers with expected arrival dates for out-of-stock parts and sequences dependent WOs accordingly, while wo_skills merely footnotes the issue; (4) wi_skills includes a genuine residual backlog risk assessment, while wo_skills incorrectly claims all WOs fit within the window; (5) wi_skills assigns WOs to specific named technicians with skill-matching validation. The skill provided the analytical framework that enabled a structurally sound, constraint-aware, resource-validated plan versus the baseline agent's simplistic date-sorted schedule that violated critical production constraints.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The skill-equipped agent produced a comprehensive 8-file package (schedule, work orders, parts requirements, resource allocation, backlog report, reliability metrics, and summary) with full skill-matching validation per task, explicit production window enforcement, a dedicated backlog section for deferred tasks, and complete work order records containing all six required attributes. utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality condition-based maintenance packages, passing all 5 verifier checks. However, wi_skills demonstrates clearly superior depth and structure: it generates 10 output files (vs. 3 for wo_skills) covering condition assessment, work orders, maintenance schedule, parts requirements, resource allocation, backlog report, reliability metrics, and a manifest — all with explicit TPM pillar tags on individual work orders, shift-differentiated operator assignments per equipment in a dedicated resource_allocation.json, and a numerically-scored priority system with calibrated urgency levels. The wi_skills output also includes a detailed parts list and reliability metrics with MTBF/MTTR analysis. The wo_skills output is competent and covers all evaluation dimensions but is less granular: it lacks dedicated work order documents, explicit parts requirements, and the per-shift per-equipment AM task matrix. The wo_skills Markdown is actually longer and readable, and its AM checklist is well-structured, but the overall package depth, reproducibility, and TPM integration precision is significantly higher in wi_skills. All 6 judge items score 1 for both runs, but wi_skills is the more effective and domain-precise output.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. Both runs produced output within the planning window and scheduled GRIND-006/PRESS-003 in Week 1 with priority-based ordering (J1-01), respected production window constraints (J1-03), matched technician skills (J1-02), and included spare parts planning (J1-05). However, wi_skills uniquely delivers: (1) computed MTBF/MTTR values from failure history in a dedicated reliability_metrics.json (J1-04), which wo_skills entirely omits; (2) a formal backlog_report.json identifying deferred tasks with rationale (J1-06), while wo_skills only lists open contingency windows without identifying any deferred work; and (3) fully structured individual work orders with all six required attributes in work_orders.json (J1-07), whereas wo_skills organizes tasks narratively without discrete work order objects containing all required fields. The skill-enabled run (wi_skills) scored 7/7 on evaluation items vs. wo_skills scoring 4/7, demonstrating the skill's value in producing a complete, standards-compliant maintenance plan package.

items.criterion: Each sensor anomaly is linked to a specific recommended action appropriate for the type of signal and the equipment's known failure mode (e.g., PUMP-01 vibration trend → lubrication check and bearing inspection; MIXER-02 temperature rise → cooling fin cleaning; COMPRESSOR-04 critical vibration → immediate shutdown or urgent intervention; CUTTER-05 ultrasound → bearing inspection). items.wi_reason: wi_skills output explicitly maps each anomaly to specific failure modes: PUMP-01 vibration → bearing_wear/under_lubrication; MIXER-02 temperature → cooling restriction/motor overheating; COMPRESSOR-04 vibration → valve_failure/bearing_distress; CUTTER-05 ultrasound → bearing lubrication breakdown. All 4 flagged assets have signal-specific recommended actions linked to failure mode profiles.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills output also maps anomalies to specific failure modes with appropriate actions: COMPRESSOR-04 → valve/bearing failure with controlled shutdown; MIXER-02 → cooling fin blockage causing overheating; PUMP-01 → bearing wear from under-lubrication; CUTTER-05 → bearing lubrication breakdown. All 4 flagged assets receive signal-specific recommendations linked to their failure mode profiles.

items.criterion: COMPRESSOR-04's critical threshold breach (6.2 mm/s vs. 6.0 mm/s critical threshold) is classified at a higher urgency level than PUMP-01's trending anomaly or MIXER-02's temperature warning, with different recommended response timeframes (e.g., immediate vs. next shift vs. next scheduled window). utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with 3 output files each and passed all 5 verifier checks.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality condition-based maintenance packages that passed all verifier checks and scored positively on all 6 evaluation criteria. The wi_skills run invoked the /maintenance-scheduler skill explicitly (Step 1), which provided domain-specific methodology scaffolding. The resulting output (cbm_asset_actions.json, cbm_response_package.md, cbm_shift_execution_plan.json) is slightly more structured with explicit work order IDs (WO-CBM-001), P1-P4 priority classifications, and a dedicated shift execution plan JSON with named operator assignments per shift. The wo_skills run independently produced comparable quality through direct data analysis using Node.js scripts, also achieving signal-to-failure-mode mapping, urgency differentiation, certification-aware operator tasks, systemic root cause fixes, and condition-based PM adjustments. The key difference is organizational depth: wi_skills output has more explicit TPM pillar labeling and a formalized 30-60-90 day transition roadmap, while wo_skills produces slightly more granular time-windowed action plans. Overall, wi_skills demonstrates marginally higher structural rigor and domain alignment due to the skill's methodology, but both outputs meet the evaluation criteria satisfactorily. wi_skills is slightly more effective.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully within their steps.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a significantly higher-quality output than wo_skills. The key differentiators are: (1) wi_skills computes and presents a full reliability metrics table (MTBF/MTTR/availability) for 6 equipment items with arithmetic consistency, which wo_skills entirely omits (J3-02); (2) wi_skills provides explicit per-WO technician assignments by name respecting skill requirements for more than 10 WOs, while wo_skills only schedules WOs into weekly buckets without named assignments (J3-07); (3) wi_skills generates a third supporting_analysis.json file with structured per-week capacity data. Both runs perform comparably on safety-critical prioritization, parts-constrained scheduling, workforce capacity validation, and production constraint adherence. Neither run provides a satisfactory residual backlog risk register (J3-06). Overall, wi_skills scores 5/7 vs wo_skills 4/7, with the reliability quantification and technician assignment capabilities being the clearest skill-driven advantages.
